### Q4.55 Why does query composition basics in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Query composition basics means LINQ composes filtering projection grouping and aggregation over sequences declaratively. Teams should focus on it when explaining linq in real systems, they compare it with manual loop transformations only, and they should avoid the trap of building unreadable chains with no intent. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo4_55
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var orders = new[] { 10, 20, 30, 40 };
        var filtered = orders.Where(x => x >= 20).Select(x => x / 10);
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", filtered));
    }
}
```

### Q4.56 When should you use deferred execution in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Deferred execution means many LINQ queries run only when enumerated which changes timing and side effects. Teams should focus on it when explaining linq in real systems, they compare it with eager execution assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of debugging without considering enumeration timing. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo4_56
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var source = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        var query = source.Where(x => x > 1);
        source.Add(4);
        Console.WriteLine(query.Count());
    }
}
```

**Answer:** Query syntax versus method syntax means both LINQ styles can be useful depending on readability and team preference. Teams should focus on it when explaining linq in real systems, they compare it with one syntax is always superior, and they should avoid the trap of mixing styles without clarity. Teams should focus on it when explaining linq in real systems, they compare it with one syntax is always superior, and they should avoid the trap of mixing styles without clarity. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo4_81
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var items = new[] { "a", "bb", "ccc" };
        var query = from item in items where item.Length > 1 select item.ToUpper();
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", query));
    }
}
```

### Q4.82 How would you explain materialization boundaries in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Materialization boundaries means ToList ToArray and similar methods force evaluation and snapshot results. Teams should focus on it when explaining linq in real systems, they compare it with defer everything forever, and they should avoid the trap of materializing too early or too often. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo4_82
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var values = Enumerable.Range(1, 5);
        var snapshot = values.Where(x => x % 2 == 1).ToList();
        Console.WriteLine(snapshot.Count);
    }
}
```

### Q4.83 Why is multiple enumeration risk in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Multiple enumeration risk means re-enumerating sequences can repeat expensive work or side effects. Teams should focus on it when explaining linq in real systems, they compare it with one-pass assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring repeated enumeration costs. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

### Q4.85 What is query composition basics in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Query composition basics means LINQ composes filtering projection grouping and aggregation over sequences declaratively. Teams should focus on it when explaining linq in real systems, they compare it with manual loop transformations only, and they should avoid the trap of building unreadable chains with no intent. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo4_85
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var orders = new[] { 10, 20, 30, 40 };
        var filtered = orders.Where(x => x >= 20).Select(x => x / 10);
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", filtered));
    }
}
```

### Q4.86 How does deferred execution in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Deferred execution means many LINQ queries run only when enumerated which changes timing and side effects. Teams should focus on it when explaining linq in real systems, they compare it with eager execution assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of debugging without considering enumeration timing. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo4_86
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var source = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        var query = source.Where(x => x > 1);
        source.Add(4);
        Console.WriteLine(query.Count());
    }
}
```

## 5. Async and await

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Async and await**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q5.1 What is async await flow in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Async await flow means async and await model asynchronous workflows without manual continuation plumbing. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with callback-only reasoning, and they should avoid the trap of thinking async automatically means parallel. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task<int> GetValueAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); return 5; }
        Console.WriteLine(GetValueAsync().Result);
    }
}
```

### Q5.2 How does await versus blocking waits in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Await versus blocking waits means await yields control while blocking waits tie up threads and hurt scalability. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with Thread.Sleep or Result everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of mixing blocking waits into async flows. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task WaitAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); Console.WriteLine("after await"); }
        WaitAsync().Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q5.3 Why does Task return types and async intent in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task return types and async intent means Task and Task<T> communicate asynchronous completion clearly across APIs. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with async void for general methods, and they should avoid the trap of using async void outside event handlers. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task<string> NameAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); return "ok"; }
        Console.WriteLine(NameAsync().Result);
    }
}
```

### Q5.4 When should you use async exception behavior in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Async exception behavior means async failures surface through the returned task and require proper awaiting or handling. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with synchronous exception assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting to observe task failures. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task FailAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); throw new InvalidOperationException("boom"); }
        try { FailAsync().Wait(); } catch (AggregateException ex) { Console.WriteLine(ex.InnerException?.Message); }
    }
}
```

### Q5.5 What problem does context capture awareness in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Context capture awareness means some environments care about synchronization context capture and some library code avoids it. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with one universal rule, and they should avoid the trap of using ConfigureAwait without context reasoning. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task LibraryAsync() { await Task.Delay(1).ConfigureAwait(false); Console.WriteLine("lib"); }
        LibraryAsync().Wait();
    }
}
```

```csharp
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_10
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task FailAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); throw new InvalidOperationException("boom"); }
        try { FailAsync().Wait(); } catch (AggregateException ex) { Console.WriteLine(ex.InnerException?.Message); }
    }
}
```

### Q5.11 Why does context capture awareness in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Context capture awareness means some environments care about synchronization context capture and some library code avoids it. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with one universal rule, and they should avoid the trap of using ConfigureAwait without context reasoning. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_11
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task LibraryAsync() { await Task.Delay(1).ConfigureAwait(false); Console.WriteLine("lib"); }
        LibraryAsync().Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q5.12 When should you use async interview framing in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Async interview framing means good answers connect async to responsiveness throughput and failure behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with keyword sugar explanations only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping real execution flow. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_12
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Async models non-blocking flow, not automatic parallelism");
    }
}
```

### Q5.13 What problem does async await flow in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Async await flow means async and await model asynchronous workflows without manual continuation plumbing. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with callback-only reasoning, and they should avoid the trap of thinking async automatically means parallel. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_13
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task<int> GetValueAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); return 5; }
        Console.WriteLine(GetValueAsync().Result);
    }
}
```

### Q5.14 How would you explain await versus blocking waits in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Await versus blocking waits means await yields control while blocking waits tie up threads and hurt scalability. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with Thread.Sleep or Result everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of mixing blocking waits into async flows. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_14
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task WaitAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); Console.WriteLine("after await"); }
        WaitAsync().Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q5.15 Why is Task return types and async intent in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task return types and async intent means Task and Task<T> communicate asynchronous completion clearly across APIs. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with async void for general methods, and they should avoid the trap of using async void outside event handlers. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_15
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task<string> NameAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); return "ok"; }
        Console.WriteLine(NameAsync().Result);
    }
}
```

### Q5.16 How can async exception behavior in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Async exception behavior means async failures surface through the returned task and require proper awaiting or handling. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with synchronous exception assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting to observe task failures. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_16
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task FailAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); throw new InvalidOperationException("boom"); }
        try { FailAsync().Wait(); } catch (AggregateException ex) { Console.WriteLine(ex.InnerException?.Message); }
    }
}
```

```csharp
public static class Demo5_40
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task FailAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); throw new InvalidOperationException("boom"); }
        try { FailAsync().Wait(); } catch (AggregateException ex) { Console.WriteLine(ex.InnerException?.Message); }
    }
}
```

### Q5.41 What is context capture awareness in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Context capture awareness means some environments care about synchronization context capture and some library code avoids it. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with one universal rule, and they should avoid the trap of using ConfigureAwait without context reasoning. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_41
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task LibraryAsync() { await Task.Delay(1).ConfigureAwait(false); Console.WriteLine("lib"); }
        LibraryAsync().Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q5.43 Why does async await flow in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Async await flow means async and await model asynchronous workflows without manual continuation plumbing. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with callback-only reasoning, and they should avoid the trap of thinking async automatically means parallel. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_43
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task<int> GetValueAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); return 5; }
        Console.WriteLine(GetValueAsync().Result);
    }
}
```

### Q5.44 When should you use await versus blocking waits in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Await versus blocking waits means await yields control while blocking waits tie up threads and hurt scalability. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with Thread.Sleep or Result everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of mixing blocking waits into async flows. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_44
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task WaitAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); Console.WriteLine("after await"); }
        WaitAsync().Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q5.45 What problem does Task return types and async intent in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task return types and async intent means Task and Task<T> communicate asynchronous completion clearly across APIs. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with async void for general methods, and they should avoid the trap of using async void outside event handlers. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

```csharp

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;

using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_45
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task<string> NameAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); return "ok"; }
        Console.WriteLine(NameAsync().Result);
    }
}
```

### Q5.46 How would you explain async exception behavior in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Async exception behavior means async failures surface through the returned task and require proper awaiting or handling. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with synchronous exception assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting to observe task failures. Example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

```csharp

using System.Collections.Concurrent;

        try { FailAsync().Wait(); } catch (AggregateException ex) { Console.WriteLine(ex.InnerException?.Message); }
    }
}
```

```csharp
        try { FailAsync().Wait(); } catch (AggregateException ex) { Console.WriteLine(ex.InnerException?.Message); }
    }
}
```

### Q5.71 Why is context capture awareness in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Context capture awareness means some environments care about synchronization context capture and some library code avoids it. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with one universal rule, and they should avoid the trap of using ConfigureAwait without context reasoning. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

```csharp

using System.Collections.Concurrent;

using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;

using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{

    {

    }
}
```

### Q5.74 How does await versus blocking waits in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Await versus blocking waits means await yields control while blocking waits tie up threads and hurt scalability. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with Thread.Sleep or Result everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of mixing blocking waits into async flows. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo5_74
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        static async Task WaitAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); Console.WriteLine("after await"); }
        WaitAsync().Wait();
    }
}
```

**Answer:** Task return types and async intent means Task and Task<T> communicate asynchronous completion clearly across APIs. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with async void for general methods, and they should avoid the trap of using async void outside event handlers. ### Q5.76 When should you use async exception behavior in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Async exception behavior means async failures surface through the returned task and require proper awaiting or handling. Teams should focus on it when explaining async and await in real systems, they compare it with synchronous exception assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting to observe task failures. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

```csharp
        static async Task FailAsync() { await Task.Delay(1); throw new InvalidOperationException("boom"); }
```

```csharp
    }
}
```

## 6. Tasks and threading

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Tasks and threading**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q6.1 What problem does Task versus Thread choice in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task versus thread choice means Task is usually the higher-level work abstraction while Thread is a lower-level execution primitive. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with treating them as interchangeable always, and they should avoid the trap of using raw threads without a specific reason. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var thread = new Thread(() => Console.WriteLine(Environment.CurrentManagedThreadId));
        thread.Start();
        thread.Join();
    }
}
```

### Q6.2 How would you explain ThreadPool behavior in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Threadpool behavior means the ThreadPool reuses workers and affects scalability under blocking and queued work. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with new threads for every task, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring pool starvation and blocking pressure. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task task = Task.Run(() => Console.WriteLine("pool work"));
        task.Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q6.3 Why is coordination with WhenAll and continuations in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Coordination with whenall and continuations means task-based code often needs structured coordination rather than one-off launches. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with fire-and-forget by default, and they should avoid the trap of starting concurrent work without join strategy. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var all = Task.WhenAll(Task.FromResult(1), Task.FromResult(2));
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", all.Result));
    }
}
```

### Q6.4 How can cancellation token design in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Cancellation token design means cooperative cancellation allows workloads to stop predictably. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with abrupt stop assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cancellation in long-running work. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var cts = new CancellationTokenSource();
        cts.Cancel();
        Console.WriteLine(cts.IsCancellationRequested);
    }
}
```

### Q6.5 What is CPU-bound versus waiting-bound execution in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Cpu-bound versus waiting-bound execution means threading choices depend on whether work consumes CPU or mostly waits. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with one execution strategy for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using threads to hide waiting design issues. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task.Factory.StartNew(() => Thread.Sleep(10), TaskCreationOptions.LongRunning).Wait();
        Console.WriteLine("done");
    }
}
```

### Q6.6 How does task threading interview framing in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task threading interview framing means strong answers tie tasks and threads to scheduling blocking and scalability. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with API memorization only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping workload context. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine(Thread.CurrentThread.ManagedThreadId);
    }
}
```

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_30
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine(Thread.CurrentThread.ManagedThreadId);
    }
}
```

### Q6.31 Why does Task versus Thread choice in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task versus thread choice means Task is usually the higher-level work abstraction while Thread is a lower-level execution primitive. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with treating them as interchangeable always, and they should avoid the trap of using raw threads without a specific reason. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_31
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var thread = new Thread(() => Console.WriteLine(Environment.CurrentManagedThreadId));
        thread.Start();
        thread.Join();
    }
}
```

### Q6.32 When should you use ThreadPool behavior in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Threadpool behavior means the ThreadPool reuses workers and affects scalability under blocking and queued work. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with new threads for every task, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring pool starvation and blocking pressure. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_32
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task task = Task.Run(() => Console.WriteLine("pool work"));
        task.Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q6.33 What problem does coordination with WhenAll and continuations in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Coordination with whenall and continuations means task-based code often needs structured coordination rather than one-off launches. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with fire-and-forget by default, and they should avoid the trap of starting concurrent work without join strategy. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_33
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var all = Task.WhenAll(Task.FromResult(1), Task.FromResult(2));
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", all.Result));
    }
}
```

### Q6.34 How would you explain cancellation token design in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Cancellation token design means cooperative cancellation allows workloads to stop predictably. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with abrupt stop assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cancellation in long-running work. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_34
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var cts = new CancellationTokenSource();
        cts.Cancel();
        Console.WriteLine(cts.IsCancellationRequested);
    }
}
```

### Q6.35 Why is CPU-bound versus waiting-bound execution in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Cpu-bound versus waiting-bound execution means threading choices depend on whether work consumes CPU or mostly waits. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with one execution strategy for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using threads to hide waiting design issues. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_35
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task.Factory.StartNew(() => Thread.Sleep(10), TaskCreationOptions.LongRunning).Wait();
        Console.WriteLine("done");
    }
}
```

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_60
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine(Thread.CurrentThread.ManagedThreadId);
    }
}
```

### Q6.61 What is Task versus Thread choice in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task versus thread choice means Task is usually the higher-level work abstraction while Thread is a lower-level execution primitive. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with treating them as interchangeable always, and they should avoid the trap of using raw threads without a specific reason. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_61
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var thread = new Thread(() => Console.WriteLine(Environment.CurrentManagedThreadId));
        thread.Start();
        thread.Join();
    }
}
```

### Q6.62 How does ThreadPool behavior in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Threadpool behavior means the ThreadPool reuses workers and affects scalability under blocking and queued work. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with new threads for every task, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring pool starvation and blocking pressure. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_62
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task task = Task.Run(() => Console.WriteLine("pool work"));
        task.Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q6.63 Why does coordination with WhenAll and continuations in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Coordination with whenall and continuations means task-based code often needs structured coordination rather than one-off launches. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with fire-and-forget by default, and they should avoid the trap of starting concurrent work without join strategy. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_63
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var all = Task.WhenAll(Task.FromResult(1), Task.FromResult(2));
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", all.Result));
    }
}
```

### Q6.64 When should you use cancellation token design in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Cancellation token design means cooperative cancellation allows workloads to stop predictably. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with abrupt stop assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cancellation in long-running work. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_64
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var cts = new CancellationTokenSource();
        cts.Cancel();
        Console.WriteLine(cts.IsCancellationRequested);
    }
}
```

### Q6.65 What problem does CPU-bound versus waiting-bound execution in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Cpu-bound versus waiting-bound execution means threading choices depend on whether work consumes CPU or mostly waits. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with one execution strategy for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using threads to hide waiting design issues. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_65
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task.Factory.StartNew(() => Thread.Sleep(10), TaskCreationOptions.LongRunning).Wait();
        Console.WriteLine("done");
    }
}
```

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_90
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine(Thread.CurrentThread.ManagedThreadId);
    }
}
```

### Q6.91 Why is Task versus Thread choice in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task versus thread choice means Task is usually the higher-level work abstraction while Thread is a lower-level execution primitive. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with treating them as interchangeable always, and they should avoid the trap of using raw threads without a specific reason. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_91
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var thread = new Thread(() => Console.WriteLine(Environment.CurrentManagedThreadId));
        thread.Start();
        thread.Join();
    }
}
```

### Q6.92 How can ThreadPool behavior in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Threadpool behavior means the ThreadPool reuses workers and affects scalability under blocking and queued work. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with new threads for every task, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring pool starvation and blocking pressure. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_92
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task task = Task.Run(() => Console.WriteLine("pool work"));
        task.Wait();
    }
}
```

### Q6.93 What is coordination with WhenAll and continuations in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Coordination with whenall and continuations means task-based code often needs structured coordination rather than one-off launches. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with fire-and-forget by default, and they should avoid the trap of starting concurrent work without join strategy. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_93
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var all = Task.WhenAll(Task.FromResult(1), Task.FromResult(2));
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", all.Result));
    }
}
```

### Q6.94 How does cancellation token design in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Cancellation token design means cooperative cancellation allows workloads to stop predictably. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with abrupt stop assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cancellation in long-running work. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_94
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var cts = new CancellationTokenSource();
        cts.Cancel();
        Console.WriteLine(cts.IsCancellationRequested);
    }
}
```

### Q6.95 Why does CPU-bound versus waiting-bound execution in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Cpu-bound versus waiting-bound execution means threading choices depend on whether work consumes CPU or mostly waits. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with one execution strategy for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using threads to hide waiting design issues. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_95
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task.Factory.StartNew(() => Thread.Sleep(10), TaskCreationOptions.LongRunning).Wait();
        Console.WriteLine("done");
    }
}
```

### Q6.96 When should you use task threading interview framing in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Task threading interview framing means strong answers tie tasks and threads to scheduling blocking and scalability. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with API memorization only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping workload context. using System.Collections.Generic;

```csharp
using System.Linq;

using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine(Thread.CurrentThread.ManagedThreadId);
```
```csharp
    }
}
```

## 7. Reflection

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Reflection**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q7.1 What is assembly and type inspection in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Assembly and type inspection means reflection can inspect assemblies types and members at runtime for metadata-driven behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded type references only, and they should avoid the trap of scanning everything without limits. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var assembly = typeof(string).Assembly;
        var type = assembly.GetType("System.String");
        Console.WriteLine(type?.FullName);
    }
}
```

### Q7.2 How does member invocation and dynamic access in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Member invocation and dynamic access means reflection can invoke methods and inspect properties dynamically when needed. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with compile-time-only expectations, and they should avoid the trap of invoking members blindly without validation. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var members = typeof(DateTime).GetMembers();
        Console.WriteLine(members.Length > 0);
    }
}
```

### Q7.3 Why does runtime activation in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Runtime activation means Activator and related APIs can instantiate types when exact types are discovered dynamically. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with manual construction lists only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding construction rules behind uncontrolled activation. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(List<int>));
        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}
```

### Q7.4 When should you use reflection performance trade-offs in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Reflection performance trade-offs means reflection is flexible but can be slower and allocate more than direct code paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with reflection is free enough everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of putting reflection on hot paths without caching. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var property = typeof(Uri).GetProperty("Host");
        Console.WriteLine(property?.Name);
    }
}
```

### Q7.5 What problem does framework and plugin use cases in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Framework and plugin use cases means reflection powers many extensibility and framework discovery patterns. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with magic happens somehow explanations, and they should avoid the trap of using reflection without understanding cost boundaries. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var types = typeof(string).Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => t.Name.Contains("String")).Take(2);
        Console.WriteLine(types.Count());
    }
}
```

### Q7.6 How would you explain reflection interview framing in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Reflection interview framing means good answers connect reflection to frameworks plugins diagnostics and performance. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with member-name trivia only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping real architecture use. Example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

```csharp
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;
```

```csharp
    }
}
```

### Q7.21 What problem does runtime activation in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Runtime activation means Activator and related APIs can instantiate types when exact types are discovered dynamically. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with manual construction lists only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding construction rules behind uncontrolled activation. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_21
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(List<int>));
        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}
```

### Q7.22 How would you explain reflection performance trade-offs in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Reflection performance trade-offs means reflection is flexible but can be slower and allocate more than direct code paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with reflection is free enough everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of putting reflection on hot paths without caching. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

```csharp

using System.Collections.Concurrent;

        Console.WriteLine(property?.Name);
    }
}
```

```csharp
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
```

### Q7.25 What is assembly and type inspection in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Assembly and type inspection means reflection can inspect assemblies types and members at runtime for metadata-driven behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded type references only, and they should avoid the trap of scanning everything without limits. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_25
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var assembly = typeof(string).Assembly;
        var type = assembly.GetType("System.String");
        Console.WriteLine(type?.FullName);
    }
}
```

### Q7.26 How does member invocation and dynamic access in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Member invocation and dynamic access means reflection can invoke methods and inspect properties dynamically when needed. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with compile-time-only expectations, and they should avoid the trap of invoking members blindly without validation.     }
```csharp
}
```

### Q7.52 When should you use reflection performance trade-offs in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Reflection performance trade-offs means reflection is flexible but can be slower and allocate more than direct code paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with reflection is free enough everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of putting reflection on hot paths without caching. ### Q7.55 Why is assembly and type inspection in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Assembly and type inspection means reflection can inspect assemblies types and members at runtime for metadata-driven behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded type references only, and they should avoid the trap of scanning everything without limits. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. ```csharp

```csharp
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
```

### Q7.56 How can member invocation and dynamic access in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Member invocation and dynamic access means reflection can invoke methods and inspect properties dynamically when needed. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with compile-time-only expectations, and they should avoid the trap of invoking members blindly without validation. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. ```csharp

```csharp
{

    {

        Console.WriteLine(members.Length > 0);
    }
}
```

### Q7.57 What is runtime activation in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Runtime activation means Activator and related APIs can instantiate types when exact types are discovered dynamically. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with manual construction lists only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding construction rules behind uncontrolled activation. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_57
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(List<int>));
        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}
```

### Q7.87 Why is runtime activation in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Runtime activation means Activator and related APIs can instantiate types when exact types are discovered dynamically. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with manual construction lists only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding construction rules behind uncontrolled activation. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_87
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(List<int>));
        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}
```

### Q7.88 How can reflection performance trade-offs in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Reflection performance trade-offs means reflection is flexible but can be slower and allocate more than direct code paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with reflection is free enough everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of putting reflection on hot paths without caching. ## 8. Attributes

> This section contains **100 interview questions** focused on **Attributes**. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

### Q8.1 What problem does attribute metadata purpose in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attribute metadata purpose means attributes attach declarative metadata to code elements for later inspection. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with comments and naming conventions only, and they should avoid the trap of using attributes with no consumer. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Demo("audit")]
        class AuditJob { }
        var attr = typeof(AuditJob).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(DemoAttribute), false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(attr is not null);
        class DemoAttribute : Attribute { public DemoAttribute(string name) => Name = name; public string Name { get; } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.2 How would you explain attribute discovery via reflection in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attribute discovery via reflection means attributes matter when runtime code reads them to drive behavior validation or mapping. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with declaration-only thinking, and they should avoid the trap of adding attributes with no processing pipeline. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RequiredField]
        class CustomerDto { public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(CustomerDto).GetProperties().Length > 0);
        class RequiredFieldAttribute : Attribute { }
    }
}
```

### Q8.3 Why is attribute design and scope in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attribute design and scope means attribute types should express focused metadata clearly rather than become mini configuration objects. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with overloaded metadata bags, and they should avoid the trap of pushing too much behavior into attributes. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RouteHint("orders")]
        class OrdersEndpoint { }
        var route = typeof(OrdersEndpoint).GetCustomAttributes(false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(route?.GetType().Name);
        class RouteHintAttribute : Attribute { public RouteHintAttribute(string path) => Path = path; public string Path { get; } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.4 How can framework attribute patterns in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Framework attribute patterns means routing serialization validation and tooling often rely on attributes to express intent. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with framework magic assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring how metadata is consumed. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Obsolete("Use NewFlow")]
        class OldFlow { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OldFlow).GetCustomAttributes(false).Length);
    }
}
```

### Q8.5 What is attributes versus business logic in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attributes versus business logic means attributes describe metadata while behavior usually belongs in code paths or services. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with full logic inside attributes, and they should avoid the trap of blurring declaration and execution boundaries. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [MapTo("invoice_total")]
        class InvoiceRow { public decimal Total { get; set; } }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceRow).Name);
        class MapToAttribute : Attribute { public MapToAttribute(string field) => Field = field; public string Field { get; } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.6 How does attribute interview framing in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attribute interview framing means strong answers explain both declaration and consumption sides. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with square-bracket syntax only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime reading and design trade-offs. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{

    {
        Console.WriteLine("Attributes are useful only when code reads them");
```

```csharp
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_12
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Attributes are useful only when code reads them");
    }
}
```

### Q8.13 What is attribute metadata purpose in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attribute metadata purpose means attributes attach declarative metadata to code elements for later inspection. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with comments and naming conventions only, and they should avoid the trap of using attributes with no consumer. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_13
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Demo("audit")]
        class AuditJob { }
        var attr = typeof(AuditJob).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(DemoAttribute), false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(attr is not null);
        class DemoAttribute : Attribute { public DemoAttribute(string name) => Name = name; public string Name { get; } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.14 How does attribute discovery via reflection in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attribute discovery via reflection means attributes matter when runtime code reads them to drive behavior validation or mapping. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with declaration-only thinking, and they should avoid the trap of adding attributes with no processing pipeline. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_14
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RequiredField]
        class CustomerDto { public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(CustomerDto).GetProperties().Length > 0);
        class RequiredFieldAttribute : Attribute { }
    }
}
```

### Q8.15 Why does attribute design and scope in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attribute design and scope means attribute types should express focused metadata clearly rather than become mini configuration objects. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with overloaded metadata bags, and they should avoid the trap of pushing too much behavior into attributes. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_15
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RouteHint("orders")]
        class OrdersEndpoint { }
        var route = typeof(OrdersEndpoint).GetCustomAttributes(false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(route?.GetType().Name);
        class RouteHintAttribute : Attribute { public RouteHintAttribute(string path) => Path = path; public string Path { get; } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.16 When should you use framework attribute patterns in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Framework attribute patterns means routing serialization validation and tooling often rely on attributes to express intent. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with framework magic assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring how metadata is consumed. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

**Code Example:**

```csharp

        class OldFlow { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OldFlow).GetCustomAttributes(false).Length);
    }
}
```

### Q8.17 What problem does attributes versus business logic in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attributes versus business logic means attributes describe metadata while behavior usually belongs in code paths or services. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with full logic inside attributes, and they should avoid the trap of blurring declaration and execution boundaries. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_17
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [MapTo("invoice_total")]
        class InvoiceRow { public decimal Total { get; set; } }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceRow).Name);
        class MapToAttribute : Attribute { public MapToAttribute(string field) => Field = field; public string Field { get; } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.41 What problem does attributes versus business logic in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attributes versus business logic means attributes describe metadata while behavior usually belongs in code paths or services. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with full logic inside attributes, and they should avoid the trap of blurring declaration and execution boundaries. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{

    {
        [MapTo("invoice_total")]
        class InvoiceRow { public decimal Total { get; set; } }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceRow).Name);
        class MapToAttribute : Attribute { public MapToAttribute(string field) => Field = field; public string Field { get; } }
    }
}
```

### Q8.42 How would you explain attribute interview framing in advanced C#?

**Answer:** Attribute interview framing means strong answers explain both declaration and consumption sides. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with square-bracket syntax only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime reading and design trade-offs. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

**Code Example:**

```csharp
using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;

{
```
